seo.title: Trat Province, Thailand — Complete Guide 2026
seo.metaDescription: Everything about Trat Province: gateway to Koh Chang, Koh Kood, and Koh Mak. Thailand's easternmost province with 52 islands, mangrove forests, and gem markets. Area: 2,819 km², population: 230,000.

overview: Trat is Thailand's easternmost province, a quiet coastal gem on the Gulf of Thailand best known as the gateway to the Koh Chang archipelago — home to Thailand's third-largest island, the pristine Koh Kood, and the laid-back Koh Mak. Beyond the islands, Trat town itself charms visitors with its atmospheric old quarter of wooden shophouses, bustling night market, and a thriving gem-trading heritage. The province borders Cambodia to the east, where the Cardamom Mountains form a natural frontier, and its 52 islands and extensive mangrove forests make it one of eastern Thailand's most ecologically rich areas. Despite its natural wealth, Trat sees far fewer tourists than southern beach destinations, offering an authentic Thai experience at a fraction of the cost.
geography: Trat covers 2,819 km² at the far eastern edge of Thailand's Gulf coast, roughly 315 km southeast of Bangkok. The province's coastline faces the Gulf of Thailand to the south and west, while the Cardamom mountain range forms the natural border with Cambodia's Koh Kong, Pursat, and Battambang provinces to the east. Offshore, 52 islands are scattered across the eastern sea, with the Mu Ko Chang Marine National Park protecting the largest cluster. Koh Chang (Elephant Island) is Thailand's third-largest island at 429 km², followed by Koh Kood (the fourth largest) to the south. The mainland features a mix of coastal plains, mangrove forests, rubber plantations, and fruit orchards. Seven districts (amphoe) divide the province: Mueang Trat, Khlong Yai, Laem Ngop, Ko Chang, Ko Kut, Bo Rai, and Khao Saming.
highlights: Koh Chang — Thailand's third-largest island with jungle-covered mountains, white-sand beaches, and waterfalls inside Mu Ko Chang National Park
Koh Kood — Thailand's fourth-largest island, known for crystal-clear waters, Klong Chao waterfall, and a peaceful atmosphere with no nightlife
Koh Mak — a small, flat island between Koh Chang and Koh Kood with a relaxed vibe, rubber plantations, and excellent snorkeling
Trat Old Town — atmospheric wooden shophouses, a famous indoor-outdoor night market, and gem shops dating back generations
Mu Ko Chang Marine National Park — a protected archipelago of over 40 islands with coral reefs, mangrove forests, and sea turtle nesting sites
Laem Ngop — the mainland ferry port to the islands, with a historic pier, seafood restaurants, and the Naval Battle Monument commemorating a 1941 engagement with France
Hat Lek Border Crossing — Thailand's easternmost border crossing to Cambodia's Koh Kong province, used by overland travelers heading to Phnom Penh or Sihanoukville
Bo Rai Gem Market — a Saturday morning market in the Bo Rai district where local miners trade rubies and sapphires mined from the surrounding hills

bestTimeToVisit.peak: Nov–Feb: Cool season with temperatures of 24–31°C, low humidity, and clear skies. Ideal for island hopping, snorkeling, and beach activities. All ferries operate on full schedule. Peak hotel prices on Koh Chang.
bestTimeToVisit.shoulder: Mar–May: Hot season with temperatures reaching 33–36°C. Seas remain calm and visibility is excellent for diving. Accommodation prices begin to drop. Songkran celebrations in Trat town are lively but uncrowded.
bestTimeToVisit.lowSeason: Jun–Oct: Monsoon season with heavy afternoon rains and rough seas. Some ferry services to Koh Kood and Koh Mak are reduced or suspended. Many resorts on smaller islands close. Koh Chang remains accessible year-round, and hotel prices drop 30–50%. Waterfalls are at their most spectacular.
bestTimeToVisit.recommendation: Visit November to February for the best weather, calm seas, and full ferry service to all islands. March and April are good for fewer crowds and lower prices while seas are still calm. Avoid the smaller islands (Koh Kood, Koh Mak) during June to October when ferries are limited.

topAttractions.name: Koh Chang (Mu Ko Chang National Park)
topAttractions.description: Thailand's third-largest island and the centerpiece of the Mu Ko Chang Marine National Park. The mountainous, jungle-covered island offers white-sand beaches (White Sand Beach, Lonely Beach, Klong Prao), dramatic waterfalls (Klong Plu, Than Mayom), excellent snorkeling and diving, and a laid-back nightlife scene. The interior is almost entirely undeveloped rainforest.
topAttractions.entranceFee: National park fee: 200 THB (~$5.70 USD) adults, 100 THB children. Ferries from mainland: 80–120 THB one-way

topAttractions.name: Koh Kood (Ko Kut)
topAttractions.description: Thailand's fourth-largest island and one of its most beautiful, located at the southern end of the archipelago near the Cambodian border. Known for pristine beaches (Klong Chao Beach, Ao Tapao), a stunning waterfall (Klong Chao Waterfall) that flows into a natural swimming pool, and a traditional fishing village on stilts at Ao Salat. Very little development and no nightlife — a true escape.
topAttractions.entranceFee: Free entry (not within the national park boundary). Ferry from Laem Ngop: 350–550 THB one-way depending on speed

topAttractions.name: Koh Mak
topAttractions.description: A small, flat island between Koh Chang and Koh Kood, about 16 km² in size. Known for its relaxed atmosphere, rubber and coconut plantations, and excellent shallow-water snorkeling at Koh Rang nearby. The island has declared itself 'low carbon' and promotes sustainable tourism. Family-run resorts and seafood restaurants line the beaches.
topAttractions.entranceFee: Free entry. Ferry from Laem Ngop: 350–450 THB one-way

topAttractions.name: Trat Night Market
topAttractions.description: An atmospheric market in the heart of Trat's old town that runs every evening along the indoor-outdoor Thanon Tat Mai shopping street. Stalls sell fresh seafood, local curries, grilled cuttlefish with red sauce, tropical fruits (durian, mangosteen, rambutan from local orchards), and Thai desserts. A favorite with locals and budget travelers.

topAttractions.name: Klong Plu Waterfall
topAttractions.description: The most accessible and popular waterfall on Koh Chang, a short hike from Klong Prao Beach on the island's west coast. The three-tiered waterfall drops into a deep natural pool perfect for swimming. Best visited in the rainy season (June–October) when water flow is strongest, though it runs year-round.
topAttractions.entranceFee: Included in Koh Chang national park fee (200 THB adults)

topAttractions.name: Naval Battle Monument (Laem Ngop)
topAttractions.description: A monument at Laem Ngop commemorating the Battle of Ko Chang on 17 January 1941, when the Royal Thai Navy engaged French Vichy naval forces in one of the few naval battles in Southeast Asian waters during World War II. The small museum tells the story of the engagement and offers views over the sea toward the islands.

topAttractions.name: Mangrove Forest Boardwalk (Ban Tha Ranae)
topAttractions.description: A community-based ecotourism project in Ban Tha Ranae offering guided kayak tours and boardwalk trails through pristine mangrove forests. The mangroves are home to mudskippers, fiddler crabs, monitor lizards, and various bird species. Boat tours through narrow mangrove channels take about 1.5 hours.
topAttractions.entranceFee: Boat tour: 150–300 THB per person depending on group size

localFood: Grilled cuttlefish with red sauce (pla meuk yang nam daeng) — freshly caught cuttlefish on skewers dipped in a special red chili sauce and grilled over charcoal
Crab curry with bamboo shoots (gaeng puu sai nor mai) — a non-soupy curry made with fresh blue crab, bamboo shoots, kaffir lime leaves, and pea aubergine
Soft-shell crab with garlic and pepper (puu nim gratiem prik Thai) — deep-fried soft-shell crab tossed in crispy garlic and cracked black pepper
Sour tamarind fish soup (tom som pla) — a tangy soup using young tamarind instead of sugar for a distinctively eastern Thai flavor
Prawn baked with glass noodles (kung op wun sen) — clay pot prawns baked with bean thread noodles, ginger, and sesame oil
Herbaceous fish salad (laab pla) — minced fresh fish mixed with mint, cilantro, shallots, roasted rice powder, and lime juice
Tropical fruit from local orchards — Trat is a major producer of durian, mangosteen, rambutan, and salak (snake fruit), sold fresh at roadside stalls and the night market
culture: Trat's culture reflects its position as a frontier province at the crossroads of Thai and Cambodian civilizations. The gem-trading heritage of Bo Rai district dates back centuries, when rubies and sapphires were mined from the hills along the Cambodian border. The old town's wooden shophouses — some over 100 years old — recall Trat's history as a busy trading port. Local festivals include the annual Trat Fruit Festival (May–June) celebrating the durian and mangosteen harvest, and the Ko Chang Music Festival. Fishing remains a core livelihood in coastal villages, and the stilted fishing village of Ao Salat on Koh Kood preserves a traditional way of life. The province's cuisine is distinctly eastern Thai, with bold, balanced flavors that rely on fresh seafood and local herbs rather than sugar — reflecting Cambodian culinary influences across the border.
gettingThere: From Bangkok, buses depart Ekkamai (Eastern) Bus Terminal hourly from 05:00 to 23:00 (around 200–280 THB, 5–6 hours). Buses from Morchit (Northern) Terminal run at 07:30, 11:00, 18:00, and 22:00 (240 THB). The Government 999 Bus goes directly to Ao Thammachat pier, convenient for Koh Chang ferries. Bangkok Airways operates daily flights from Suvarnabhumi Airport to Trat Airport (TDX) in about 1 hour (2,000–4,000 THB). By car, take Highway 3 (Sukhumvit Road) east — the distance is approximately 315 km. From Trat town or the airport, songthaews shuttle passengers to the ferry piers at Laem Ngop for boats to Koh Chang, Koh Mak, and Koh Kood.
gettingAround: On the mainland, songthaews (shared pickup trucks) are the main public transport, running between Trat town, Laem Ngop pier, and nearby districts for 30–60 THB. Motorbike taxis and tuk-tuks are available in Trat town. On Koh Chang, songthaews run along the west coast road between the ferry pier and Lonely Beach (50–100 THB per trip), and motorbike rental is the most popular way to explore (200–300 THB/day). Car rental is available on Koh Chang but roads are steep and winding in the interior. On Koh Kood and Koh Mak, most resorts arrange transfers and motorbike rental is available (200–250 THB/day). Inter-island ferries connect Koh Chang, Koh Mak, Koh Wai, and Koh Kood during high season.
travelTips: Book ferries to Koh Kood and Koh Mak in advance during high season (Nov–Feb) as services can sell out, especially on weekends
The Hat Lek border crossing to Cambodia is open daily 07:00–20:00 — bring a passport photo for the Cambodian visa on arrival (around $30 USD)
Koh Chang's west coast has the beaches and nightlife; the east coast is quieter with fishing villages and mangroves
Bring cash — ATMs are available in Trat town and on Koh Chang's west coast, but smaller islands have limited banking
May–June is durian season in Trat — visit the fruit orchards around Bo Rai for fresh durian at farm-gate prices
Mosquito repellent is essential, especially in mangrove areas and during the rainy season
If visiting Bo Rai gem market (Saturday mornings), go early — trading starts at dawn and winds down by 10:00
Download offline maps before visiting smaller islands as mobile signal can be patchy on Koh Kood and Koh Mak

sources.name: I Am Koh Chang — Island Guide
